text: Hello, this is a robotic distal pancreatectomy for a solid pseudocapillary neoplasm in a young male with BMI of fifty-five. I try to do robotic distal pancreatectomy the same way every time, same stepwise manner.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac and mobilizes the stomach away, creating space for further dissection.
The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum and dissects the short gastric vessels, revealing the tumor located in the pancreas.
Unless I'm doing a spleen preserving, then I don't need to take all the short gastric. But if I'm doing a splenectomy, I take all the short gastric so I don't have to worry about them later.
The video shows a large tumor located in the proximal body of the pancreas.
The surgeon manipulates the splenic flexure, which appears challenging to mobilize. After completing the dissection of the short gastric vessels, the spleen is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the left crus of the diaphragm in the surgical field, marking the end of this part of the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon removes adhesions overlying the tumor located in the proximal body of the pancreas, revealing clearer access to the tumor site.
The surgeon sutures the stomach to the abdominal wall, providing retraction and adequate exposure of the pancreas without the need for a liver retractor.
The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to locate the vessels surrounding the large tumor in the proximal body of the pancreas.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas away from the transverse colon and splenic flexure, isolating the pancreas-spleen complex. The inferior part of the pancreas is carefully separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, starting from lateral to medial, with the flexure extending across the spleen.
The surgeon prepares for a splenectomy due to the presence of a bulky tumor encasing the vessels, which makes spleen preservation challenging.
I guess I could have done a spleen preserving with resection of the vessels.
This is a plant fracture, keeps going all the way up. I needed a skilled colorectal surgeon to mobilize the splank flexure for me. Redundant splank flexure there.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the omega loop, separating it entirely from the omentum.

The surgeon has mobilized the surrounding structures, allowing the pancreas to become visible. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is observed joining nearby, and the surgeon begins searching for the splenic vein.
The surgeon isolates the splenic vein and splenic artery, dividing them before proceeding to divide the pancreas and detach its lateral attachments.

The surgeon uses a combination of hook Maryland and vessel sealer to manipulate the splenic vein, with visible branches extending to the pancreas. The splenic vein is divided along with the pancreas using a stapler, while the splenic artery is isolated and ligated separately.
The surgeon carefully isolates the proximal section of the pancreas to prevent injury to the adjacent portal vein.
The surgeon navigates above the pancreas, revealing the coronary vein. The bulky tumor in the area limits exposure to the underlying splenic artery.
The surgeon moves to the right side to separate the pancreatic neck from the hepatic artery, aiming to expose the portal vein situated above.
The surgeon plans to divide the pancreas over the portal vein, with difficulty noted in exposing the origin of the splenic artery due to its challenging location.

The surgeon is close to the tumor, with limited access due to the coronary vein obstructing the view. A tunnel is created, similar to the Whipple procedure, and the pancreas is stapled first to improve visibility for dissecting the artery.
The surgeon isolates the pancreatic neck from the hepatic artery and proceeds to divide the pancreas using a surgical stapler with a blue load, ensuring protection with a seam guard.
The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to divide the pancreas at the neck, where the tissue is visibly thinner, revealing the underlying portal vein.
The surgeon mobilizes the body and tail of the pancreas to the left after dividing the pancreatic neck, which aids in exposing the origin of the splenic artery.

The surgeon uses the third arm to lift the pancreas to the left, exposing the splenic vein clearly. The vein is visible, but the surgeon refrains from stapling it until the splenic artery is controlled.

The surgeon exposes the splenic artery, which is pulsating visibly near the tumor. The proximity of the tumor to the artery's origin makes isolation challenging without prior division of the pancreas.
The surgeon clips and staples the pancreas, taking precautions to prevent bleeding and pseudoaneurysm formation between the staples.
The surgeon uses a white load stapler for the vein and continues to mobilize the pancreas and spleen from the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon points to the tumor, identified as a five-centimeter solid pseudopapillary neoplasm, with surrounding tissue showing no signs of malignancy in twelve lymph nodes and clear margins.
The surgeon places a drain near the surgical site and removes the remaining portion of the spleen, completing the procedure.
And that's it. Thank you for watching.
